channel.title: Executive Protection by City on Close Protection and Security Services | CloseProtectionHire.com
channel.description: Recent content in Executive Protection by City on Close Protection and Security Services | CloseProtectionHire.com

item.title: Executive Protection in Alexandria, Egypt
item.description: Alexandria’s business case is different from Cairo’s, and executive protection planning follows that difference closely. The city handles the large majority of Egypt’s seaborne trade and hosts six refineries and three petrochemical complexes, a meaningful share of national refining capacity. Executive visits here skew toward energy, shipping, and industrial business, with site access to port or petrochemical facilities operating under active security protocols and photography restrictions that extend to mobile phones. A protection team working this corridor needs advance site clearance arranged before the executive arrives, not on the day.

item.title: Executive Protection in Brasilia, Brazil
item.description:
Corporate visits to Brasilia have a different centre of gravity than Sao Paulo or Rio de Janeiro. Rather than banking, industry, or tourism, the pull here is government relations, regulatory affairs, and diplomatic engagement, and the Setor de Embaixadas Sul and Norte, the purpose-built diplomatic quarter housing most of Brazil’s foreign missions, is where a large share of executive protection work actually happens.

The threat picture reflects that specialisation. The US State Department’s Level 2 advisory for Brazil (2026 update) applies nationally, citing violent crime and kidnapping risk, but Brasilia’s Plano Piloto runs below Sao Paulo and Rio de Janeiro on most of those measures. What Brasilia does carry, distinctly, is government-district risk: on 13 November 2024, a lone attacker set off explosives at the Praca dos Tres Poderes, outside the Supreme Federal Court. He died. No one else was hurt. It’s a low-frequency risk, and not one that shows up in a standard national crime rating, which is exactly why it needs naming directly rather than folded into generic guidance.

item.title: Executive Protection in Canberra, Australia
item.description: Executive protection in Canberra runs on a different client base to Australia’s larger cities. This is the federal capital, purpose-built after the 1911 Griffin design competition rather than grown up around trade or industry, and its principal work reflects that: government contracts, public-sector executives, and diplomatic-adjacent business dominate over the finance-sector and retail-crowd settings that define Sydney or Melbourne assignments. The Australian Public Service based in the ACT numbered 70,221 in 2025, around 35 per cent of the entire federal workforce according to the 2025 State of the Service figures, and Canberra also hosts over 100 foreign embassies and high commissions, more than any other Australian city, per the National Capital Authority.

item.title: Executive Protection in Chiang Mai, Thailand
item.description:
Chiang Mai’s executive protection work has a different rhythm to Bangkok’s. There’s no political-cycle risk to track and no terrorism precedent, so most assignments centre on Nimmanhaemin’s business and co-working district or the eastern ring road corridor, both manageable environments with straightforward vehicle access. The city functions as a regional hub for logistics, remote-work companies and smaller-scale corporate gatherings rather than the large national-capital events Bangkok sees.

What genuinely shapes a Chiang Mai detail is the calendar. Burning season, roughly mid-February through April, pushes PM2.5 well past WHO guidance across the region; a 29 March 2026 reading of 188 micrograms per cubic metre, cited in coverage of that year’s crisis, sat at more than four times the guideline. Any executive stationed in Chiang Mai during those months, whether for a short visit or a longer posting, needs air-quality monitoring built into the daily schedule rather than treated as background noise. Outdoor meetings, site visits or hospitality events need indoor contingency options ready for the worst-air days.

item.title: Executive Protection in Medan, Indonesia
item.description: Executive visits to Medan tend to look different from those to Jakarta or Surabaya, and that difference starts with the economy. North Sumatra is one of Indonesia’s largest palm oil producing regions, and Medan is where the companies operating those plantations, including Asian Agri, are based. A Medan trip therefore more often includes a plantation or mill site visit in the surrounding regencies than a single office meeting, and that provincial leg needs its own route and timing plan rather than an extension of city-centre coverage.

item.title: Executive Protection in Pretoria, South Africa
item.description: Pretoria’s executive protection profile is shaped by what the city is: South Africa’s administrative capital, seat of the Presidency at the Union Buildings, and home to the largest concentration of foreign embassies on the continent. A meaningful share of the principal movement here is government-adjacent or diplomatic rather than purely commercial, which shifts venue access and liaison requirements away from a standard corporate protection framework and toward one that shares more with diplomatic-security practice.

item.title: Executive Protection in Rabat, Morocco
item.description:
Ministries, not motorcycle thieves, set the tone for most executive visits to Rabat. Corporate travel here more often means a ministry meeting, an embassy function, or a government-linked engagement than the finance-sector schedule typical of Casablanca. That changes what a protection detail spends its time on: checkpoint navigation, diplomatic liaison, and access protocol matter more than street-crime awareness, though the latter hasn’t vanished entirely.

Souissi, the diplomatic quarter housing most of Morocco’s foreign embassies, is the clearest example. It carries a heavier, more visible state security presence than anywhere in Casablanca, and each mission runs its own screening for visitors. A protection team attending an embassy function there coordinates with the host’s security office in advance; turning up and expecting a standard corporate walk-in doesn’t work. The same caution extends to the government quarter around parliament and the royal palace at Dar al-Makhzen, both symbolic-value sites that shape Moroccan security posture and, by extension, route planning.

item.title: Executive Protection in Saint Petersburg
item.description:
Executive protection for Saint Petersburg is a programme decision, not a booking, and the first item in that programme is the FCDO’s advisory: against all travel to Russia, a country-wide position that gives Saint Petersburg no exemption on account of its museums or its imperial history. Anyone weighing a visit needs to treat that advisory as the starting input for planning, not a line to note and move past.

Saint Petersburg adds a specific complication of its own on top of the national picture. Both the British and US consulates general in the city closed in 2018, and neither has reopened. A Moscow-based executive at least has an embassy to call on; a Saint Petersburg-based one does not, and any escalation runs through the Embassy in Moscow or the Consulate in Ekaterinburg instead. That gap belongs in the risk assessment from the outset, alongside the detention risk the FCDO names explicitly for British nationals across the whole country, and the fact that Saint Petersburg has itself suffered a fatal terrorist attack, the April 2017 metro bombing that killed 15 people.

item.title: Executive Protection in Sharjah, UAE
item.description:
Sharjah’s executive protection demand looks different from Dubai’s from the outset: rather than DIFC boardrooms and Palm Jumeirah villas, a meaningful share of Sharjah visits are manufacturing, trading, and logistics business centred on the Sharjah Airport International Free Zone (SAIF Zone), a free zone established in 1996 that now serves more than 2,900 companies. A protection programme built for Dubai’s HNWI and corporate culture does not automatically transfer.

The legal environment is the sharpest planning difference. Sharjah bans alcohol outright, the only UAE emirate to do so, and enforces its Decency and Public Conduct Rules, issued by the Government of Sharjah on 16 June 2001, with more consistency than Dubai applies its comparable norms. For a programme built around a principal’s comfort and compliance as much as physical safety, this belongs in the pre-visit brief from the start, not as a footnote.

item.title: Executive Protection in Valparaiso, Chile
item.description:
Executive protection demand in Valparaiso rests on a fact Santiago simply doesn’t share: since March 1990, this port city, not the capital, has been the seat of Chile’s National Congress. Legislative-sector visits, government-relations engagements, and policy business bring a different class of principal here than the mining and financial-services executives typical of a Santiago trip, and the Congreso Nacional district functions as its own institutional security zone accordingly.

That said, the city’s general risk profile still needs proper attention. FCDO Chile travel advice (2026) documents muggings occurring in Valparaiso, sometimes in daylight, and names it alongside Santiago as a location for large-scale protests, which can cluster around legislative sitting dates given the Congress’s presence. The UNESCO-listed heritage hills of Cerro Alegre and Cerro Concepcion carry a comparatively calm daytime profile built on tourist footfall, while the port district and lower old-town streets see more of the documented crime. Terrain matters too: Valparaiso’s narrow, steep hillside streets limit close-quarters movement options in a way flatter cities don’t, and Chile’s genuine seismic history, including major earthquakes affecting the city in 1906 and 1985, makes street-level evacuation planning a real, not decorative, part of any extended engagement.

item.title: Executive Protection in Aarhus, Denmark
item.description: Maersk, Vestas, Arla Foods, Salling Group, Jysk: Denmark’s second city carries an outsized share of the country’s corporate headquarters for a place its size, and that concentration shapes what executive protection in Aarhus actually looks like day to day. Most engagements are less about the street and more about getting a principal cleanly through a sequence of credentialed sites, from the Port of Aarhus, which handles over half the nation’s container traffic, to the Agro Food Park life-sciences campus and the Katrinebjerg IT cluster near the university.

item.title: Executive Protection in Charleroi, Belgium
item.description: Charleroi is a city of two very different security profiles depending on where the itinerary actually lands. Gosselies, home to aerospace-structures manufacturer Sonaca and the A6K digital-innovation hub, backed by EU Recovery and Resilience Plan funding and expanding to 10,000 learners a year from autumn 2026, is genuine economic redevelopment with a materially lower-risk profile than the wider city. Most executive visits happen here rather than in the historic centre, and treating Gosselies as its own planning zone, rather than folding it into a general Charleroi risk picture, produces a more accurate assessment.

item.title: Executive Protection in Cordoba, Spain
item.description:
Cordoba holds a record most cities would rather not: 47.4C, recorded at nearby La Rambla on 14 August 2021, Spain’s hottest temperature on file according to AEMET. That is not a curiosity for a protection plan, it is an operating condition. Verified vehicle air conditioning, hydration routines, and shade at any outdoor stop belong in the itinerary for principals visiting in summer, particularly those unused to Andalusian heat.

Most executive visits to Cordoba sit within a narrower band than in Spain’s larger cities: agricultural business, the region’s oleotourism circuit, and cultural or heritage engagements around the Mezquita-Catedral and the Juderia. That district also carries the city’s densest tourist foot traffic and its most consistently flagged pickpocketing risk, which is the practical close-quarters consideration for most Cordoba details rather than anything more severe. Spain’s national terrorism alert has held at Level 4 of 5 since November 2015, and the FCDO advises vigilance in crowded places across the country, a backdrop that applies here as it does everywhere in Spain. May brings a sharper spike: the UNESCO-listed Patios Festival and the Feria de Cordoba fill the old town for the entire month, and route planning needs to account for that well ahead of the date.

item.title: Executive Protection in Gdansk, Poland
item.description: On 31 August 1980, Lech Walesa and Deputy Prime Minister Mieczyslaw Jagielski signed the Gdansk Agreement, following a shipyard strike that began 14 August, and Solidarity registered as a trade union that November. That history is not a museum piece here: the European Solidarity Centre keeps it active, and Gdansk remains a genuine, recurring destination for institutional, diplomatic, and commemorative visits whose security and access needs sit apart from standard corporate travel. Around August anniversary dates in particular, official events and a stronger security presence near the Centre are more likely, and confirming current arrangements ahead of a mid-August visit is worth the call.

item.title: Executive Protection in Hannover, Germany
item.description: More than 4,000 exhibitors from over 70 countries pass through Hannover Messe each year, and Deutsche Messe AG describes it in industry terms as the world’s largest industrial trade fair. Fair week is when executive protection in Hannover is at its busiest, with principal movement concentrated almost entirely on the exhibition grounds rather than the wider city, and a team that knows the venue’s scale and access patterns from experience, not a first visit, keeps transfers efficient.

item.title: Executive Protection in Katowice, Poland
item.description: Katowice used to mean coal and steel. It now also means the Katowice Special Economic Zone, established in 1996, which has pulled in over 50 billion zloty of investment, created more than 100,000 jobs, and was named Europe’s best SEZ in 2024 by fDi Intelligence, part of the FT group. Business-services employment inside the zone grew roughly 70% over four years to around 27,000 workers, and that shift toward financial services, IT, and BPO work is what most executive visits to Katowice are actually built around now, alongside the region’s remaining industrial base.

item.title: Executive Protection in Namur, Belgium
item.description: Namur has been Wallonia’s official capital since 1986, a status the Parliament of Wallonia confirmed formally in 2010, and that political weight shapes the city’s visitor profile more than anything else. The Parliament of Wallonia and the Government of Wallonia, the Elysette, draw a steady stream of political, institutional, and diplomatic visitors, and their security and access needs sit apart from standard corporate travel. During sitting weeks, that means confirming access arrangements and security presence around the government quarter through the host institution well before arrival rather than treating it as an ordinary city visit.

item.title: Executive Protection in Nuremberg, Germany
item.description: Spielwarenmesse alone brings around 2,800 exhibitors from roughly 60 countries and some 67,000 visitors through NürnbergMesse each year, and it is one of three major fairs, alongside BrauBeviale and Consumenta, that shape what executive protection in Nuremberg actually involves. For most principals visiting the city, the job is less about the wider streets and more about credentialed access: hospitality suites, delegation transfers, and opening events coordinated with the exhibition centre’s own security team well before arrival.

item.title: Executive Protection in Rouen, France
item.description: Rouen’s numbers tell an unusual story for a city of its size. French Ministry of Interior data from April 2024 placed it 4th of 368 cities over 22,500 residents for recorded crime per capita, ahead of both Lyon and Paris, though the Ministry itself frames that as informational rather than a full risk measure. In practice, standard travel security, vetted transport, and awareness around the main railway station give most corporate visitors an appropriate baseline. Higher-profile principals, given the ranking, may reasonably want closer protection.

item.title: Executive Protection in Wroclaw, Poland
item.description: Google, HP, Nokia, IBM, UBS, Volvo IT: Wroclaw’s 234 modern business-service centres, counted by KPMG Poland as of May 2025, put it among Central Europe’s denser BPO and shared-services hubs, and that shapes what executive protection here actually looks like. Most visits concentrate around business park districts, particularly the Bielany Wroclawskie corridor and shared-services campuses, rather than the tourist-focused Rynek old town, so the working pattern is business-campus-to-hotel rather than sightseeing with a security tail.

item.title: Executive Protection in Antwerp, Belgium
item.description: Antwerp runs on two industries that shape almost every executive itinerary here: diamonds and cargo. The Antwerp World Diamond Centre still carries the memory of its 2003 vault heist, and access into the Diamond District reflects that history with tight, deliberate control rather than open pedestrian flow. A short distance away, the Port of Antwerp-Bruges anchors the largest integrated petrochemical cluster in Europe, drawing a steady stream of industrial and supply-chain visitors who need site accreditation before they can set foot past the gate.

item.title: Executive Protection in Bremen, Germany
item.description:
Bremen’s economy runs on wings and cargo. Airbus Bremen, the second-largest Airbus production site in Germany at roughly 450,000 square metres and over 4,000 staff, sits alongside the Bremen-Bremerhaven port complex, one of the larger integrated seaport systems in Europe. Both draw a steady flow of aerospace and logistics executives whose itineraries hinge on facility access rather than city-centre meetings.

The security picture is unusually calm. Bremen sits under the same BKA nationwide terrorism assessment as the rest of Germany, without any elevated targeted threat specific to corporate visitors. Gröpelingen, in the western part of the city near the port, and the Bahnhofsvorstadt around the main station both see somewhat higher recorded crime than Bremen’s residential districts, but this rarely bears on a well-planned aerospace or port-sector visit. What does matter more practically is the thinner consular footprint: with no standalone US consular agency in Bremen since 2018, crisis planning routes through Berlin for both British and American principals.

item.title: Executive Protection in Dusseldorf, Germany
item.description:
Dusseldorf makes its name at Messe Dusseldorf, host to more than 50 international trade fairs a year from boot to ProWein to MEDICA, and along Immermannstrasse, where several hundred Japanese companies have built a genuine business community locals call Little Tokyo. Executive visits here tend to orbit one of those two poles, either exhibition-hall meetings or Japanese-community engagements requiring interpreter support.

Corporate risk is low, though not featureless. The BKA has held an elevated national terrorism assessment since the 2016 Berlin Christmas market attack, a picture that covers all of Germany rather than singling out Dusseldorf, while the BfV separately flags industrial and corporate espionage as a live concern, one that connects directly to Messe-linked meeting security given Dusseldorf’s exhibition-hub role. Practical, everyday risk sits closer to home: petty theft at the Hauptbahnhof, nightlife-district opportunism in the Altstadt, and, once a year, the sheer scale of Rosenmontag carnival, which brings several hundred thousand people through the Altstadt and Konigsallee.

item.title: Executive Protection in Leipzig, Germany
item.description:
Leipzig’s corporate visitor traffic runs on two tracks: automotive manufacturing at BMW Group Plant Leipzig and Porsche Leipzig, and a trade-fair tradition at Leipziger Messe that stretches back to 1165. Both bring predictable, scheduled crowds rather than the open pedestrian flow of a typical European city centre, which shapes how protection teams plan movement here.

The threat picture is generally low, sitting under the same nationwide BKA terrorism assessment as the rest of Germany. Connewitz is worth flagging specifically: the district has a documented history of clashes between demonstrators and police, including an hours-long containment operation after the 2023 verdict in the Lina E. case. This is a political and localised pattern, not one aimed at business travellers, but it warrants a route check for any itinerary passing through. More routine is the petty-crime pattern at the Hauptbahnhof concourse, one of the largest station floor areas in Europe, and the Promenaden shopping complex beneath it.

item.title: Executive Protection in Liege, Belgium
item.description:
Liege earns its keep from logistics rather than tourism. Liege Airport (LGG) is the fifth-largest cargo airport in Europe by volume, running around the clock behind ASL Airlines Belgium, yet most visiting executives still land at Brussels or Charleroi and travel in by road. Steel and manufacturing visits, along with events at the Palais des Congres, round out the rest of a typical corporate itinerary here.

Liege’s risk profile sits a step above several smaller Belgian cities: recorded crime runs higher, and Sainte Marguerite is specifically flagged as a district to avoid after dark. None of this amounts to a targeted threat against business travellers, but it changes how closely a detail plans evening movement compared with a quieter Belgian centre. Add to that a strong industrial and trade-union heritage, which brings more frequent strike activity than some neighbouring cities, and a national OCAM/CUTA threat level that has sat at 3 of 4 through 2026, and the overall picture calls for slightly tighter route and timing discipline than a straightforward low-risk assignment.

item.title: Executive Protection in Lille, France
item.description: Lille trades on its location. Eurostar and TGV lines run directly to Paris, Brussels, and London through Lille Europe and Lille Flandres stations, making the city as much a transfer point as a destination for a European corporate itinerary. Euralille, the business district that grew up around those stations, now hosts around 150 companies, while retail and logistics names including Auchan and Decathlon sit within a short drive of the centre.

item.title: Executive Protection in Montpellier, France
item.description:
Montpellier has quietly built one of France’s more interesting tech and life-sciences profiles outside Paris. French Tech Montpellier has held Capital status since 2019, IBM has kept a site running here since 1965, and a genuine biotech cluster, anchored by Sanofi manufacturing and research operations, gives the city a corporate identity that outpaces its size.

Security-wise, Montpellier sits under France’s national Vigipirate posture without any city-specific terrorism elevation. What deserves closer attention is more mundane: France Bleu and Herault Tribune reported in 2026 on organised pickpocket and card-theft networks working Gare Saint-Roch, with an arrest that year confirming the activity, so station transfers get planned with that specifically in mind. La Paillade, officially a Zone de Securite Prioritaire, sits well outside the Ecusson, Antigone, and biotech-cluster areas a corporate itinerary would normally touch, and national strike activity, though quieter since the 2025 pension reform suspension, is still worth checking against travel dates.

item.title: Executive Protection in Palma de Mallorca, Spain
item.description:
Palma runs on money that arrives by water. The superyacht and marina economy anchored by the Real Club Nautico de Palma and STP Shipyard Palma draws an international ownership and brokerage clientele, while Son Vida, up in the hills above the city, houses a portion of the HNWI principals who base themselves in Palma for longer stretches of the season.

FCDO Spain (2024) is specific about the practical risks here: organised distraction-theft teams work the airport, cruise terminal, and cathedral-Born area, drink spiking including GHB cases occurs in bars and clubs, and recurring anti-tourism demonstrations, running under the banner Menys Turisme, Mes Vida since 2024, have continued into 2025 and 2026 with occasional temporary road closures. None of it amounts to a targeted threat against corporate visitors, but each element earns a specific line in the movement plan rather than a generic mention. Magaluf, worth noting for delegations expecting it to be part of the same trip, sits in the separate Calvia municipality some 15km away and is not covered by a Palma-based engagement.

item.title: Executive Protection in Strasbourg, France
item.description:
Strasbourg carries a weight few cities its size do: it hosts European Parliament plenary sessions roughly monthly, alongside the permanent presence of the Council of Europe, the European Court of Human Rights, and the European Pharmacopoeia. That institutional density defines the corporate and diplomatic visitor profile here more than almost anywhere else covered on this site.

It also carries the memory of 11 December 2018, when a gunman attacked the city’s Christmas market, killing five people and wounding eleven, including an Italian journalist who had been covering the European Parliament, before being killed by police two days later after a manhunt involving roughly 700 officers. Vigipirate has run at its highest tier nationally since. That history shapes planning around the Christkindelsmarik market today, which now draws close to two million visitors across more than 300 chalets each winter, and around European Parliament session weeks, which bring both heavier delegation traffic and, at times, demonstrations near the Parliament buildings. Neuhof and Hautepierre, on the city’s periphery, carry documented elevated crime levels and sit outside the typical institutional visitor’s route.

item.title: Executive Protection in Stuttgart, Germany
item.description:
Stuttgart’s corporate visitor profile is set by two names: Mercedes-Benz Group AG, headquartered in Untertürkheim, and Porsche AG, headquartered in Zuffenhausen. Add Messe Stuttgart, positioned beside the airport around 12km south of the centre and host to trade events including AMB and Blechexpo, and the shape of a typical Stuttgart itinerary becomes clear before a single meeting is scheduled.

Risk here is low, but not without texture. The BfV’s flagged concern around industrial espionage in Germany’s automotive sector applies directly to executives moving between manufacturer headquarters and supplier meetings, a consideration that shapes information discipline as much as physical protection. Two large annual events, the Cannstatter Volksfest and the Schlossplatz Christmas market, bring crowd-density challenges rather than targeted threats, and the long-running Stuttgart 21 construction project has changed pedestrian flow around the Hauptbahnhof enough that route planning treats the station as a moving target rather than a fixed layout.

item.title: Executive Protection in Bilbao, Spain
item.description: Executive protection in Bilbao is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against the Basque political context and demonstration-linked disruption: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and TIP-certified secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management rather than ad-hoc cover.

item.title: Executive Protection in Bologna, Italy
item.description: Executive protection in Bologna is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against trade-fair congestion and student-demonstration disruption: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and Prefettura-licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management rather than ad-hoc cover.

item.title: Executive Protection in Bordeaux, France
item.description: Executive protection in Bordeaux is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against civil demonstrations and HNWI-targeting crime: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and CNAPS-licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management rather than ad-hoc cover.

item.title: Executive Protection in Bridgetown, Barbados
item.description: Executive protection in Bridgetown is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against tourist and HNWI-targeted crime and hurricane-season contingencies: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and SIA-Barbados licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management.

item.title: Executive Protection in Dammam, Saudi Arabia
item.description: Executive protection in Dammam is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against regional conflict and drone risk within a strict legal and social framework: advance work on venues and routes, a written threat assessment, an operations controller, and Saudi-licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management.

item.title: Executive Protection in Eindhoven, Netherlands
item.description: Eindhoven is the Netherlands’ high-technology capital, home to ASML (the world’s largest manufacturer of photolithography systems, headquartered in adjacent Veldhoven), NXP Semiconductors, and the Philips Innovation Campus. The AIVD annual report (2023) explicitly identifies the Dutch semiconductor and high-tech sector as a target of foreign state actor intelligence operations, making Eindhoven unique among low-threat European cities in carrying a documented counter-surveillance consideration for senior technology executives. The baseline security environment is low-risk: the FCDO Netherlands travel advice (2024) records petty theft in city centres as the primary concern for general business travellers.

item.title: Executive Protection in Florence, Italy
item.description: Executive protection in Florence is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against pickpocketing and distraction-theft exposure in dense tourist areas: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and Prefettura-licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management.

item.title: Executive Protection in Genoa, Italy
item.description: Executive protection in Genoa is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against complex urban terrain and port and cruise-terminal risk: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and Prefettura di Genova licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management rather than ad-hoc cover.

item.title: Executive Protection in Ghent, Belgium
item.description: Ghent is East Flanders’ industrial and university capital, home to ArcelorMittal steel operations, Volvo Cars Belgium, and a significant pharmaceutical cluster including UCB and Ablynx/Sanofi at the Ghent campus. The city is also internationally known for the Gentse Feesten, a ten-day July festival that attracts up to one million visitors (City of Ghent data), making crowd management planning a distinctive requirement for any principal movement during that period. The security environment is low-risk: the FCDO Belgium travel advice (2024) records petty theft as the primary concern for business travellers.

item.title: Executive Protection in Izmir, Turkey
item.description: Executive protection in Izmir is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against terrorism awareness and earthquake-risk contingencies: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and Turkish National Police licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management rather than ad-hoc cover.

item.title: Executive Protection in Las Palmas, Spain
item.description: Executive protection in Las Palmas is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against pickpocketing and mountain-road driving risk on the Canary Islands: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and SES-licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management rather than ad-hoc cover.

item.title: Executive Protection in Malaga, Spain
item.description: Executive protection in Malaga is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against scooter-assisted theft and seasonal crowd-concentration risk: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and SES-licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management rather than ad-hoc cover.

item.title: Executive Protection in Male, Maldives
item.description: Executive protection in Male is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against gang violence and knife crime alongside political instability: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and Ministry of Home Affairs regulated secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management.

item.title: Executive Protection in Maracaibo, Venezuela
item.description: Executive protection in Maracaibo is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies when it owes a duty of care to an executive facing kidnapping and infrastructure and economic collapse: advance work on venues and routes, a written threat assessment, an operations controller, and secure transport from a long-established, verifiable provider integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a visit, where provider selection is the critical factor.

item.title: Executive Protection in Medina, Saudi Arabia
item.description: Executive protection in Medina is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against access restrictions, crowd density, and strict religious and social law: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and Saudi-licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a visit, where compliance with local restrictions is a leading design factor.

item.title: Executive Protection in Nantes, France
item.description: Executive protection in Nantes is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against civil demonstrations and vehicle-crime exposure: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and CNAPS-licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management rather than ad-hoc cover.

item.title: Executive Protection in Nassau, Bahamas
item.description: Executive protection in Nassau is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against violent crime and armed robbery, with hurricane-season contingency planning: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and BSIRA-licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management.

item.title: Executive Protection in Rennes, France
item.description: Rennes is Brittany’s capital and a significant hub for the French telecoms, defence, and agri-food sectors, with major employers including Orange, Technicolor, and Thales Systemes Aeroportes. Its Gare de Rennes TGV connection to Paris Montparnasse (1h25) makes it an accessible destination for senior executives on multi-city French itineraries. The security environment is low-risk: the FCDO France travel advice (2024) records petty theft as the primary concern. The specific operational consideration for Rennes is its demonstration history: the city was among the most affected by the 2023 pension reform protests, recorded by the French Interior Ministry, and route pre-planning on protest days is a standing requirement for city-centre movement.

item.title: Executive Protection in Utrecht, Netherlands
item.description: Utrecht is the Netherlands’ geographic and rail centre, anchored by Utrecht Centraal (the country’s busiest rail junction) and home to major financial institutions including Rabobank headquarters and a.s.r. insurance at the Papendorp business park. The Jaarbeurs convention centre, adjacent to Centraal station, hosts major Dutch trade fairs and conventions throughout the year. The security environment is low-risk: the FCDO Netherlands travel advice (2024) records petty theft as the primary concern for business travellers. The practical operational consideration for Utrecht is its absence of a commercial airport: all principals arrive via Amsterdam Schiphol (AMS, 40 minutes by road) or Eindhoven Airport (EIN, 40 minutes by road), making dual-airport transfer management a standard planning component.

item.title: Executive Protection in Zaragoza, Spain
item.description: Zaragoza is Aragon’s capital and a strategically positioned logistics and automotive hub, home to the PLAZA logistics platform (one of Europe’s largest inland logistics centres) and the Stellantis Figueruelas automotive plant 40km to the west. The city’s Delicias AVE station connects Madrid (1h25) and Barcelona (1h40) by high-speed rail, making it a transit node for multi-city executive itineraries. The security environment is low-risk: the FCDO Spain travel advice (2024) records petty theft and vehicle crime as the primary concerns for business travellers, with no elevated threat to executives specifically.

item.title: Executive Protection in Ouagadougou
item.description:
Executive protection in Ouagadougou is not a deployable programme in the conventional sense; the highest FCDO and US State Department risk rating, active insurgency, and mass displacement rule out standard commercial deployment. This page covers what a duty-of-care approach actually requires: specialist Sahel conflict-environment operators, a documented decision framework for whether any Burkina Faso-linked travel proceeds at all, and case-by-case assessment for essential purposes only, not a standard itinerary.

For regional staging and operational management of Burkina Faso interests, Abidjan in Cote d’Ivoire is the primary hub. Accra provides the alternative regional base with direct OUA connections.

A Hargeisa EP programme covers pre-advance of the HGA transfer and all confirmed venues, counter-surveillance from airport collection, movement security within the city, after-dark movement protocols, and medevac planning via Nairobi. For the full security picture, see our Hargeisa city briefing and security drivers in Hargeisa for vetted transport alongside executive protection.

item.title: Executive Protection in Khartoum
item.description: Executive protection in Khartoum is not a deployable programme under current conditions; Sudan’s civil war and the FCDO’s advise-against-all-travel rating rule out routine executive movement. This page covers what a duty-of-care programme actually looks like here: remote risk assessment, continuous situation monitoring, and a documented decision framework for whether any Sudan-linked travel is undertaken at all. Where interests persist, planning is built around Port Sudan and confirmed evacuation arrangements, not a standard itinerary.

item.title: Executive Protection in Kabul
item.description:
Executive protection in Kabul is not a deployable programme in the conventional sense; the FCDO and US State Department advise against all travel to Afghanistan. This page covers what a duty-of-care approach actually requires here: Taliban-approval pathways, IS-KP threat monitoring, and a documented decision framework for whether any Afghanistan-linked travel proceeds at all, given the absence of Western consular support. Planning is individual and pre-authorised, not templated.

The Fundamental Shift Since 2021

Executive protection in Kabul before August 2021 operated within a recognisable international framework: Western diplomatic presence, ANDSF security forces, civil licensing for private security companies, and access to international MEDEVAC and extraction resources. All of that framework has either gone or been fundamentally altered. The governing authority now controls all security operations, the consular safety net does not exist, and the threat environment has been reshaped by IS-KP’s active campaign.

item.title: Executive Protection in Naypyidaw
item.description:
Executive protection in Naypyidaw is a programme built for Myanmar’s most controlled operating environment, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies for essential-presence organisations with a government-liaison function: checkpoint management, detention-risk awareness, an operations controller, and evacuation-readiness planning, integrated with the itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a visit to the purpose-built capital, where restrictive conditions set the framework.

The Naypyidaw security environment

The UK FCDO advises against all travel to large parts of Myanmar and against all but essential travel to most remaining areas, reflecting the ongoing armed conflict between the SAC military and the People’s Defence Force and ethnic armed organisations. While Naypyidaw’s status as the SAC’s administrative capital insulates it from the direct conflict affecting Sagaing Region, Kayah State, and other peripheral areas, the city operates under pervasive military surveillance and checkpoint control that shapes every aspect of principal movement.
